sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 69, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: BREAKTHROUGH! Left green moved all the way to the right panel at (30-32, 45-47) with W center. Right green c at (30-32, 30-32). Right dark outline at (26-30, 44-48). Left green is just below the right dark outline.
- Plan: Move left green UP to absorb the right dark outline. Then check if level is complete.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 68 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 2.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The UP move didn't change anything. The left green is still at (30-32, 45-47) and the right dark outline is still at (26-30, 44-48). The left green didn't move UP.
Wait, the left green is at (30-32, 45-47) and the right dark outline is at (26-30, 44-48). The right dark outline's bottom is at row 30, and the left green's top is at row 30. So they overlap at row 30.
Actually, looking at the right dark outline boundary: [[26, 44], [26, 48], [30, 48], [27, 48], [26, 47], [26, 45], [27, 44], [30, 44]]. The bottom of the outline is at row 30, cols 44 and 48 (corners). The left green is at (30-32, 45-47).
So the left green is at rows 30-32, cols 45-47. The right dark outline is at rows 26-30, cols 44-48. The left green is BELOW the right dark outline, with the top of the left green (row 30) touching the bottom of the right dark outline (row 30).
But the UP move didn't work. Why? Maybe the left green can't move UP because it's blocked by the right dark outline?
Wait, but earlier when the left green was in the left panel, it could move UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT freely. Why can't it move UP now?
Oh, maybe the left green can only move in the right panel if it's not blocked. The right dark outline is above it, blocking UP movement.
Let me try moving the left green DOWN, LEFT, or RIGHT to see if it can move in the right panel.
Actually, let me think about this differently. The left green is at (30-32, 45-47) in the right panel. The right dark outline is at (26-30, 44-48). The left green is below the right dark outline.
If I move the left green LEFT, it would go to (30-32, 42-44), which is to the left of the right dark outline. Then I could move UP to (27-29, 42-44), which is to the left of the right dark outline. Then RIGHT to enter the right dark outline.
But wait, the right dark outline is a hollow square. To absorb it, I need to move the green INTO the hollow center of the outline.
The right dark outline is at (26-30, 44-48). The hollow center would be at (27-29, 45-47). The left green is at (30-32, 45-47), which is below the hollow center.
If I could move the left green UP by 1 row, it would be at (29-31, 45-47), which partially overlaps with the hollow center (27-29, 45-47). But the UP move didn't work.
Let me try a different approach. What if I need to move the left green to the LEFT first, then UP, then RIGHT to enter the right dark outline from the side?
Actually, let me first check if the left green can move in the right panel at all. Let me try LEFT.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: UP didn't work - left green blocked by right dark outline above. Left green W at (30-32, 45-47), right dark outline at (26-30, 44-48). Need to find a way to get the left green inside the right dark outline.
Plan: Try moving left green LEFT to go around the right dark outline, then UP, then RIGHT to enter from the side.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Left green at (30-32, 45-47), try moving LEFT
result = action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After LEFT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 15:
        print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
print("  level_completed:", result.get('level_completed'))

# If LEFT worked, try more LEFT then UP then RIGHT
if not result.get('level_completed'):
    # Try LEFT, UP, RIGHT sequence
    result2 = action(['LEFT', 'UP', 'RIGHT'])
    seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
    print("\nAfter LEFT, UP, RIGHT:")
    for n in seg2['nodes']:
        if n['pixels'] <= 15:
            print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
    print("  level_completed:", result2.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
